---
title: "Numerical Methods"
description: "How exact mathematical objects turn into finite-precision computations, and how conditioning, stability, and error analysis decide whether those computations can be trusted."
page-type: module-overview
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: numerical-methods
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/single-variable-calculus/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Understand why numerical methods need a language for floating point, conditioning, and stability."
  - "Know the intended first-pass order for the numerical-methods module."
  - "Connect matrix computations, approximation, and error control to the rest of the site's advanced stack."
applications:
  - scientific-computing
  - optimization
  - engineering-systems
keywords:
  - numerical methods
  - floating point
  - conditioning
  - stability
  - scientific computing
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
stable-sources:
  - "MIT 18.335J Introduction to Numerical Methods"
  - "Cornell CS4220 Numerical Analysis"
  - "Stanford CME108 scientific computing"
current-sources:
  - "MIT 18.335J course page"
  - "Cornell CS4220 introduction"
  - "Stanford CME108 bulletin"
---

## Why This Module Matters

Pure mathematics usually presents objects as exact:

- a matrix is exactly `A`
- a solution is exactly `x`
- a derivative is exactly `f'(x)`
- an eigenvalue is exactly `\lambda`

Numerical methods asks what survives when those objects are computed on an actual machine.

That shift changes the questions.

Now we care about:

- floating-point approximation
- cancellation and roundoff
- conditioning of the underlying problem
- stability of the algorithm
- approximation error versus discretization error
- whether an answer is accurate enough for the use case, not merely symbolic

This module is the bridge from exact mathematical language to trustworthy computation.

::: {.meta-strip}
::: {.meta-box}
**Prerequisites**
[Linear Algebra](../../foundation/linear-algebra/index.qmd) and [Single-Variable Calculus](../../foundation/single-variable-calculus/index.qmd) should come first. [Multivariable Calculus](../../foundation/multivariable-calculus/index.qmd), [Optimization](../optimization/index.qmd), and [Matrix Analysis](../matrix-analysis/index.qmd) make the later pages much more useful, but they are not required to start the opening page.
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Unlocks**
Scientific computing, solver literacy, error analysis, numerical linear algebra, stability language for ODEs and control
:::
::: {.meta-box}
**Research Use**
Reading papers that discuss conditioning, backward stability, iterative solvers, approximation error, discretization, or computational tradeoffs
:::
:::

## First Pass Through This Module

The intended first-pass spine for this module is:

1. [Floating-Point, Conditioning, and Backward Error](concepts/floating-point-conditioning-and-backward-error.qmd)
2. [Numerical Linear Systems and Factorizations](concepts/numerical-linear-systems-and-factorizations.qmd)
3. [Iterative Methods and Preconditioning](concepts/iterative-methods-and-preconditioning.qmd)
4. [Numerical Least Squares and Regularization](concepts/numerical-least-squares-and-regularization.qmd)
5. [Eigenvalue and SVD Computation](concepts/eigenvalue-and-svd-computation.qmd)
6. [Approximation, Differentiation, Integration, and Error Control](concepts/approximation-differentiation-integration-and-error-control.qmd)

This six-page first-pass spine is now complete. Together, it covers numerical error language, direct and iterative linear-system methods, least squares, regularization, spectral computation, and the approximation viewpoint behind differentiation, integration, and practical error control.

One optional extension page pushes the module toward dynamics and simulation:

- [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd)

## How To Use This Module

Read this module as a computation layer rather than as a replacement for exact math.

The default reading path is:

1. start with [Floating-Point, Conditioning, and Backward Error](concepts/floating-point-conditioning-and-backward-error.qmd)
2. continue to [Numerical Linear Systems and Factorizations](concepts/numerical-linear-systems-and-factorizations.qmd)
3. continue to [Iterative Methods and Preconditioning](concepts/iterative-methods-and-preconditioning.qmd)
4. continue to [Numerical Least Squares and Regularization](concepts/numerical-least-squares-and-regularization.qmd)
5. continue to [Eigenvalue and SVD Computation](concepts/eigenvalue-and-svd-computation.qmd)
6. continue to [Approximation, Differentiation, Integration, and Error Control](concepts/approximation-differentiation-integration-and-error-control.qmd)
7. if you want the ODE and simulation bridge, continue to [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd)
8. use nearby live pages in [Optimization](../optimization/index.qmd), [Matrix Analysis](../matrix-analysis/index.qmd), and [High-Dimensional Statistics](../high-dimensional-statistics/index.qmd) whenever a page talks about conditioning, spectra, or estimation error

The module should stay focused on a compact first-pass toolkit:

- finite precision
- conditioning and stability
- core linear algebra computations
- approximation and error control

## Core Concepts

- [Floating-Point, Conditioning, and Backward Error](concepts/floating-point-conditioning-and-backward-error.qmd): the opening page that explains finite precision, forward versus backward viewpoints, and why conditioning multiplies numerical trouble.
- [Numerical Linear Systems and Factorizations](concepts/numerical-linear-systems-and-factorizations.qmd): the page where exact linear algebra becomes LU, QR, and Cholesky computation.
- [Iterative Methods and Preconditioning](concepts/iterative-methods-and-preconditioning.qmd): the page where direct factorization gives way to stationary iterations, Krylov intuition, and preconditioning.
- [Numerical Least Squares and Regularization](concepts/numerical-least-squares-and-regularization.qmd): the bridge from overdetermined fitting problems to projection methods, QR/SVD computation, and Tikhonov-style stabilization.
- [Eigenvalue and SVD Computation](concepts/eigenvalue-and-svd-computation.qmd): the page where eigenvalues and singular values stop being only exact decompositions and become numerical targets with their own iterative algorithms.
- [Approximation, Differentiation, Integration, and Error Control](concepts/approximation-differentiation-integration-and-error-control.qmd): the page where interpolation, finite differences, quadrature, and adaptive error control turn approximation into an explicit numerical design problem.
- [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd): the optional extension page that turns local approximation ideas into explicit and implicit ODE solvers, stability regions, and stiffness intuition.

## Proof Patterns In This Module

- `Backward-error view`: reinterpret a computed answer as the exact answer to a nearby problem.
- `Conditioning amplifies perturbations`: separate the sensitivity of the problem from the quality of the algorithm.
- `Error decomposition`: split total error into approximation, roundoff, and iteration or discretization pieces.

## Applications

### Scientific Computing

This is the module where exact mathematics becomes implementable computation with accuracy and cost tradeoffs.

### Optimization And Statistics

Least squares, Hessian methods, covariance estimation, and spectral algorithms all depend on conditioning and solver behavior.

### Engineering And Dynamics

Later ODE, control, and simulation pages need numerical stability language, not only exact differential-equation theory.

## Go Deeper By Topic

The main starting path is:

1. [Floating-Point, Conditioning, and Backward Error](concepts/floating-point-conditioning-and-backward-error.qmd)
2. [Numerical Linear Systems and Factorizations](concepts/numerical-linear-systems-and-factorizations.qmd)
3. [Iterative Methods and Preconditioning](concepts/iterative-methods-and-preconditioning.qmd)
4. [Numerical Least Squares and Regularization](concepts/numerical-least-squares-and-regularization.qmd)
5. [Eigenvalue and SVD Computation](concepts/eigenvalue-and-svd-computation.qmd)
6. [Approximation, Differentiation, Integration, and Error Control](concepts/approximation-differentiation-integration-and-error-control.qmd)

If you want the bridge from numerical methods into ODEs, dynamics, or control, the next live page is:

7. [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd)

The strongest adjacent pages are:

- [Optimization](../optimization/index.qmd)
- [Matrix Analysis](../matrix-analysis/index.qmd)
- [High-Dimensional Statistics](../high-dimensional-statistics/index.qmd)
- [Multivariable Calculus](../../foundation/multivariable-calculus/index.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this module are:

- [MIT 18.335J: Introduction to Numerical Methods](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-335j-introduction-to-numerical-methods-spring-2019/) - official current MIT OCW course page covering floating point, conditioning, stability, and core numerical linear algebra. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Cornell CS4220: Numerical Analysis](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/courses/cs4220/2026sp/lec/2026-01-21.html) - official current course introduction describing the scientific-computing viewpoint of solving continuous-math problems fast enough and accurately enough. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CME108 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1056641) - current official course description showing a numerical-methods path that connects error analysis, linear systems, optimization, and ODEs. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Study Order

For the current module state, read:

1. [Floating-Point, Conditioning, and Backward Error](concepts/floating-point-conditioning-and-backward-error.qmd)
2. [Numerical Linear Systems and Factorizations](concepts/numerical-linear-systems-and-factorizations.qmd)
3. [Iterative Methods and Preconditioning](concepts/iterative-methods-and-preconditioning.qmd)
4. [Numerical Least Squares and Regularization](concepts/numerical-least-squares-and-regularization.qmd)
5. [Eigenvalue and SVD Computation](concepts/eigenvalue-and-svd-computation.qmd)
6. [Approximation, Differentiation, Integration, and Error Control](concepts/approximation-differentiation-integration-and-error-control.qmd)

and then, if you are heading toward simulation or ODEs,

7. [Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability](concepts/time-stepping-for-odes-and-stability.qmd)

before trying to reason casually about solver output, residuals, or floating-point implementations elsewhere on the site.

You are ready to move deeper into this module when you can:

- explain why finite precision is a modeling issue, not only a programming nuisance
- distinguish forward error, backward error, and residual
- explain why conditioning belongs to the problem while stability belongs to the algorithm
- explain why a backward-stable algorithm can still produce a poor forward answer on an ill-conditioned problem
- explain why numerical linear systems are usually solved through factorizations rather than through an explicit matrix inverse
- explain why iterative methods become attractive for large sparse systems and why preconditioning changes convergence behavior
- explain why least squares should usually be computed through QR or SVD thinking rather than by blindly forming normal equations
- explain why eigenvalue and singular-value computations need dedicated iterative ideas rather than just symbolic decomposition formulas
- explain how truncation or discretization error joins roundoff error in differentiation, integration, and interpolation
- explain why time stepping for ODEs adds a new concern: stability over many steps, not only local approximation at one step

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.335J: Introduction to Numerical Methods](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-335j-introduction-to-numerical-methods-spring-2019/) - `First pass` - official current numerical-methods course page with the right opening emphasis on accuracy and efficiency. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Cornell CS4220: Numerical Analysis](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/courses/cs4220/2026sp/lec/2026-01-21.html) - `First pass` - official current course introduction with a clean scientific-computing framing. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CME108 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1056641) - `Second pass` - official course description showing how the module naturally grows into interpolation, integration, optimization, and ODEs. Checked `2026-04-25`.
